ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general coffee beverage class; no specific 'iced café au lait' term exists. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers coffee with milk; no specific iced variant code exists. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for iced café au lait; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of iced café au lait is approximately 350 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as sold at coffee shops and cafés.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beverages, coffee, brewed, prepared with tap water (FDC ID 171890, SR Legacy) and Milk, whole, 3.25% milkfat (FDC ID 171265, SR Legacy), blended 50:50 by weight to model café au lait
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Iced café au lait is modelled as an approximately equal-volume blend of brewed black coffee and whole cow's milk, consistent with the classic café au lait preparation. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for brewed coffee (FDC ID 171890) and whole milk (FDC ID 171265) were blended at 50:50 by weight to derive per-100 g values. The input record's grabfood-sourced values are broadly consistent with this USDA-derived model and have been superseded by it. All nutrient values are sourced exclusively from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Milk, full-fat (GI 39) used as analogous estimate for the milk-and-coffee blend.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for iced café au lait in the University of Sydney GI Database. The beverage is approximately 50% whole milk and 50% brewed coffee by volume. Brewed black coffee contains negligible carbohydrate and does not materially affect GI. The GI of the beverage is therefore estimated from full-fat milk (GI 39, low) as the sole carbohydrate-contributing component, per Atkinson et al. 2008 published in Diabetes Care and indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (39 × 9.8) / 100 ≈ 3.8, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 2.8 × 3.5 = 9.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (23 mg/100 g); no added sugar in standard preparation
hypertension.guidance: Iced café au lait is suitable for individuals with hypertension when prepared without added sugar or flavoured syrups, as its sodium content is very low at 23 mg/100 g and the naturally occurring lactose from milk (2.5 g/100 g) is well below the 5 g/100 g beverage sugar threshold; however, caffeine may transiently raise blood pressure and intake should be moderated to 1–2 cups per day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 39); carbohydrate 2.8 g/100 g; sugar is naturally occurring lactose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Iced café au lait has a low glycemic index of approximately 39 and a glycemic load of only 3.8 per 350 g serving, making it appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes when consumed without added sugar or sweetened syrups; the milk protein (1.7 g/100 g) further moderates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g); low sodium (23 mg/100 g); beneficial polyphenols from coffee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Iced café au lait presents a low cardiovascular risk profile with saturated fat at 1.0 g/100 g, sodium at 23 mg/100 g, and trans fat at 0.05 g/100 g; the chlorogenic acid polyphenols in coffee are associated with modest cardioprotective effects, and moderate consumption of 1–2 cups daily is consistent with cardiovascular health guidelines when prepared without added sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory coffee polyphenols; low saturated fat; calcium from milk
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Iced café au lait may be consumed by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; coffee provides chlorogenic acid with anti-inflammatory properties, milk contributes calcium (57 mg/100 g) relevant to bone protection in patients on corticosteroids, and saturated fat is low at 1.0 g/100 g; low-fat milk may be substituted to further reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Café Au Lait is a chilled coffee beverage prepared by combining equal parts freshly brewed coffee and cold whole milk, then serving the mixture over ice. The drink originates from French café culture but has been widely adopted across Japanese coffee shop chains and Southeast Asian café menus, where it is commonly listed as a signature cold beverage. The flavour profile is mild and creamy, with the bitterness of brewed coffee balanced by the natural sweetness and richness of whole milk; the ice dilutes the beverage slightly as it melts, producing a lighter finish. Per 100 g, iced café au lait provides approximately 34 kcal, 1.7 g protein, 1.7 g fat (of which 1.0 g is saturated), and 2.8 g carbohydrate, predominantly as naturally occurring lactose from milk. Sodium is very low at 23 mg/100 g. The beverage has a low glycemic index of approximately 39 and a glycemic load of only 3.8 per standard 350 g cup, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Coffee contributes chlorogenic acid polyphenols with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, while milk provides calcium (57 mg/100 g), riboflavin, vitamin B12, and phosphorus. The drink is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-compatible in standard preparation. It is not suitable for individuals with lactose intolerance or dairy allergies. Clinically, it is appropriate for hypertension and type 2 diabetes management when consumed without added sugar or flavoured syrups, and its low saturated fat and sodium profile support cardiovascular health guidelines.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for iced café au lait exists in any approved primary source. Values are derived from a 50:50 blended model of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for brewed coffee (FDC ID 171890) and whole milk (FDC ID 171265), which is consistent with the standard café au lait preparation. The input record's grabfood-sourced values are broadly consistent with this model. Confidence is medium because the exact coffee-to-milk ratio may vary by vendor and preparation style.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from a 50:50 blend of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy brewed coffee (FDC ID 171890) and whole milk (FDC ID 171265); no approved primary source entry exists for iced café au lait specifically. All nutrient evidence is sourced exclusively from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — no forbidden sources have been used. The input record listed 'Japanese' as the primary cuisine; this has been retained and the mandatory Japanese local-language name (アイスカフェオレ) has been added to satisfy G6. The country_of_origin field in the input was 'Singapore', which is inconsistent with the 'Japanese' cuisine tag; the cuisine classification has been retained as Japanese per the input record's ethnic_tags, and region_of_origin is set to Japan accordingly. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for brewed coffee and whole milk blend; neither coffee nor milk contains dietary fibre. Vitamin C is 0.0 mg/100 g: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this beverage type. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all populated from the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy blended model. GI is estimated from full-fat milk (GI 39) per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), indexed in the University of Sydney GI Database, as brewed coffee contributes negligible carbohydrate. The original source entry in the input record cited 'https://foodo.sg/' as an HPB Singapore URL but listed it only as supporting 'menu_listing'; this source has not been used for nutrient values as it does not constitute an approved nutrient evidence source for this record.
